honoree.name: Lewis And Clark
honoree.summary: William Clark was born on August 1, 1770, in Caroline County, Virginia, and died on September 1, 1838, in St. Louis, Missouri. He was an American explorer, soldier, and cartographer who co-led the historic Lewis and Clark Expedition from 1804 to 1806. He later served as the Governor of Missouri and as Superintendent of Indian Affairs. His explorations and diplomatic work significantly shaped the early expansion and relations with Native American nations of the United States.

honoree.name: Donald J. Ruhl Medal Of Honor
honoree.summary: Donald J. Ruhl was a United States Marine who served during World War II. He was born on October 14, 1921, in Kansas and posthumously received the Medal of Honor for his actions during the Battle of Iwo Jima. Ruhl was killed in action on June 10, 1945, on the island of Iwo Jima. His sacrifice is remembered as a significant example of bravery in Marine Corps history.

honoree.name: Ivan Doig
honoree.summary: Ivan Doig was an American author known for his historical novels set in the American West. He was born on June 27, 1939, in White Sulphur Springs, Montana. Doig had a distinguished career as a writer and academic, earning recognition for works like 'This House of Sky'. He died on April 9, 2015, in Seattle, Washington, leaving a significant literary legacy.

honoree.name: Senator Conrad Burns
honoree.summary: Conrad Burns was an American politician who served as a United States Senator from Montana. He was born on January 25, 1935, in Gallatin, Missouri, and died on April 28, 2016, in Billings, Montana. Burns was a Republican who served three terms in the Senate from 1989 to 2007, making him the longest-serving Republican senator from Montana. He was also a former radio station manager and was known for his work on agricultural and telecommunications issues.

honoree.name: Minnie Spotted-Wolf
honoree.summary: Minnie Spotted-Wolf was a Native American woman born on July 21, 1910, in Heart Butte, Montana. She was a member of the Blackfeet Nation and became the first Native American woman to serve in the United States Marine Corps Women's Reserve during World War II. Her service involved working as a heavy equipment operator and driver, breaking gender and cultural barriers. She is remembered as a trailblazer for Native American women in the military.

honoree.name: Jeannette Rankin
honoree.summary: Jeannette Rankin was an American politician and women's rights activist born on June 11, 1880, in Missoula, Montana Territory. She was the first woman elected to the U.S. House of Representatives, serving two non-consecutive terms. A dedicated pacifist, she was the only member of Congress to vote against U.S. entry into both World War I and World War II. She left a lasting legacy as a pioneer for women in politics and a champion for peace.

honoree.name: Otto Fossen
honoree.summary: Undersheriff Otto Fossen was shot and killed by a hitchhiker he had picked up. Donald L. Bujok, age 21, of Roundup, Montana stole a car east of Chester and proceeded to flee west on Highway 2. Local law enforcement had been alerted and as the Sheriff Dodd Keith was setting up a roadblock, Undersheriff Otto Fossen drove to Joplin to investigate a car stopped on the highway. The events of Undersheriff Fossen's attack is unclear since there were no witnesses, but it was confirmed that Undersheriff Fossen was shot five times in the neck and body and managed to get off two shots as he attempted to subdue the attacker. The suspect managed to escape in the Undersheriff’s car and proceeded west at a high rate of speed. Undersheriff Fossen was still alive when local residents found him and took him directly to the hospital where he was pronounced dead on arrival. The suspect continued to flee, but left the car in a field and continued on foot.  A manhunt was organized and approximately 200 volunteers searched the area. After an exhaustive search the suspect, Bujok, surrendered. A disassembled revolver was found in his hiding spot, and after an x-ray cartridges were found in his intestinal tract.  When the cartridges were recovered and examined by forensics the evidence was conclusive that he had been wounded by Undersheriff Fossen's weapon. Bujok was convicted and sentenced to life in prison.
